results.question: Did Theo always refuse to buy reissues, or did his position on this change over time?
results.gold_answer: Theo's position started as strong skepticism and hardened into categorical refusal. In Session 1 he expressed doubt but not a ban ('reissues are a gamble'). In Session 2, he critiqued Rashida's reissue purchases for their mastering but had not yet announced a personal rule. In Session 3, he bought an expensive new Coltrane reissue himself, found the mastering to be terrible, and declared: 'Nothing with a digital chain in the mastering is worth buying. I'm done. Originals only from now.' The 'Never again' after describing it as a waste makes clear this is a permanent policy change. By Session 5, his position is unqualified: 'Originals only, Rashida. That is the whole point.'
results.predicted_answer:
His position definitely hardened over time. On 2025-04-14 he was still treating reissues as a gamble rather than a hard no — "Regardless of the art, the mastering is where it matters. Reissues are a gamble." Then on 2025-04-21 he bought a Coltrane reissue himself, found the mastering terrible, and declared: "No. Nothing with a digital chain in the mastering is worth buying. I'm done. Originals only from now." From that point on (2025-04-28 and 2025-05-05) he held the hard line — when Rashida brought two reissues to Camden he said "Originals only, Rashida. That is the whole point. If I'm carrying a record home I want a pressing with some history in it." Preet even called him out on the shift: "one decent haul and Theo is suddenly willing to praise a record fair."

So no — he didn't always refuse. He was skeptical from the start but converted to originals-only after a bad personal experience with a reissue.
